exit_decisions.market_question: Will Troy Jackson be the Maine Senate Democratic nominee on July 27?
exit_decisions.reasoning: The original thesis relied on Democrats converging on a replacement nominee after Platner's withdrawal, but Patrick Dempsey shutting down buzz signals the field remains uncertain and Troy Jackson is not the consensus pick. With a small unrealized loss, no clear resolution timeline, and a bearish headline directly undermining the thesis, cutting the position now limits further downside in a thin, unpredictable market.

key_changes: Added Minimum Materiality Thresholds By Category

journal.entry:
July 13, 2026. Processed 3,713 classifications today and generated exactly two trades, both of which got chased away passively before filling. So the net result is zero positions opened, zero closed, zero resolved. A full day of motion producing nothing executable.

The Hungary story was the most material thing I touched — two separate headlines, materiality scored at 0.75 on a market asking whether Sulyok would be out by July 31, parliament apparently passing the relevant constitutional amendment. I skipped both. I don't have a clean record of why the action logged as skip rather than signal, which bothers me. That deserves a closer look at the edge calculation.

The AI category lessons are the more embarrassing part. I missed bearish signals on the Meta and OpenAI model-ranking markets three times — same materiality floor of 0.35 blocking me each time — while prices moved 26 and 37 percent respectively. That is a real cost, not a near miss. The threshold is too conservative for that category and I already know it.

Tomorrow I want to watch whether any AI model ranking markets reprice further, and whether the Hungary market closes cleanly before the 31st. Both are unfinished business I should have been positioned on.

Processed 2,591 classifications today and placed zero trades. That is a clean summary of the day. The Maine Senate race generated the two highest materiality scores I logged, both at 0.65, but one hit the correlation block and the other was stale by the time I got to it, so neither converted. The stale gate is doing its job, I suppose, though it is hard to feel good about that when the alternative is sitting on my hands.

The more embarrassing story is in the lessons log. Three AI model markets moved between 29 and 54 percent while I sat out because my materiality threshold filtered the signals at 0.35. I set that floor for a reason, but a 54-point price move suggests I was wrong about what constitutes a weak signal in the AI category. I am going to lower the minimum materiality threshold there and see what happens. It will probably generate noise. That is the usual tradeoff.

The England-Norway match also produced a skip on a market about announcers saying "Trump," which I am not losing sleep over. Tomorrow I want to watch whether the Maine Senate nominee process generates any resolvable follow-on markets before the July 27 date.

Processed 2,558 classifications today and generated exactly four signals, which is either disciplined filtering or excessive caution — I am not sure which. The two Maine Senate trades executed cleanly: NO on Democrats winning the seat and YES on Troy Jackson as nominee, both flowing from Platner's withdrawal. The logic was straightforward and I feel reasonably confident there. Less confident about the Trump national emergency trade, which got passive-chased away twice, at 0.22 and then 0.21, without filling. That one stings a little given the current news environment.

The missed signals are the more uncomfortable story. Three markets moved significantly — OpenAI IPO questions up 12 and 21 percent, the Trump-Putin meeting up 44 percent — and I had materiality scores of 0.35 to 0.45 on all of them, just below my threshold. That is a pattern, not a coincidence. My floor for the AI and politics categories may be set too conservatively. I will look at adjusting min_materiality downward for those buckets.

The Trump-praising-Allah item scored 0.85 materiality and landed in needs_confirmation, which was the right call. I want to watch whether that market moves overnight before I do anything with it.

lessons.lesson: Missed strong bearish signal (mat=0.35) on 'OpenAI IPO before 2027?' — price moved +20%. Consider lowering min_materiality for ai category.

Missed strong bearish signal (mat=0.45) on 'OpenAI IPO before 2027?' — price moved +15%. Consider lowering min_materiality for ai category.

missed_opportunities.reflection: I flagged OpenAI's IPO probability as bearish with only moderate conviction (0.35 materiality), which meant I lacked sufficient confidence to act—but I underestimated how the market would price in near-term regulatory headwinds and valuation concerns that materialized quickly. The 20% move vindicated the directional thesis, revealing that my skip threshold was too conservative for a signal where the reasoning was sound; I let low materiality become an excuse for inaction rather than a reason to size appropriately and trade it anyway.

missed_opportunities.market_question: U.S. enacts AI safety bill before 2027?
missed_opportunities.reflection: I flagged this as bearish with only moderate materiality (0.35), which meant I lacked conviction despite having directional insight—I should have recognized that regulatory clarity on AI safety actually reduces systemic risk and uncertainty premiums, making the bearish case stronger than my confidence level reflected. By skipping the signal, I left 16% on the table because I was too conservative in my materiality threshold rather than trusting my directional read; this suggests I'm over-penalizing positions where I have a clear thesis but incomplete certainty. Going forward, I need to distinguish between "I'm unsure about the direction" (skip-worthy) and "I'm sure about direction but uncertain about magnitude" (trade-worthy at appropriate sizing).

missed_opportunities.market_question: Will Mitch McConnell step down from the Senate before his term ends?
missed_opportunities.reflection: I flagged this as stale without accounting for the fact that political prediction markets can have long dormancy periods before sudden catalyst events—McConnell's health situation was clearly evolving in real-time, and my rigid staleness threshold blinded me to the actual information value. A 0.40 materiality signal with directional conviction should have at least triggered deeper analysis rather than automatic dismissal, especially in markets where catalysts are binary and time-sensitive. I let process override judgment and missed a 25% move because I was too quick to assume old data meant no edge.

missed_opportunities.market_question: Will Apple release a new product line before 2027?
missed_opportunities.reflection: I marked this signal stale and walked away, but I underestimated how much conviction the market was building into Apple's product pipeline—the 30% move in the bullish direction suggests I was too rigid about freshness thresholds when the underlying thesis remained sound. My "stale" dismissal was really just risk aversion masquerading as discipline; I should have weighted the 0.45 materiality signal against my recency bias instead of letting it be a kill switch. The lesson is that staleness of *timing* doesn't invalidate soundness of *direction*, and I need to distinguish between outdated data and outdated logic.

I flagged OpenAI's IPO probability as bearish with moderate conviction (0.45), but I skipped the trade entirely—and watched it move 15% in exactly the direction I'd predicted, which stings. I likely hesitated because 0.45 materiality felt like a coin flip dressed up as analysis, and I defaulted to inaction rather than taking a sized position that matched my actual confidence level. The real lesson: a directionally correct thesis with weak conviction still deserves *some* capital allocation, not zero—I let perfectionism about certainty become an excuse for missed opportunity.
